text: The surgeon uses a KII Fios optical entry trocar for initial ETEP access, insufflating while entering. The retromuscular view is visible after pneumodissection and blunt dissection, with trocars placed to clear the retrorectus space. A thorough dissection of the patient's left retromuscular space is performed before docking the robot.

The surgeon docks the robotic system and begins the crossover technique, incising the posterior rectus sheath one centimeter lateral to the linea alba to expose a cuff of fascia. The large preperitoneal fat pocket is visible, facilitating the retro muscular repair. The absence of an upper abdominal incision simplifies the crossover procedure.

The surgeon identifies the contralateral rectus muscle and incises the posterior sheath to enter the retro-rectus space. Care is taken to avoid incising into the linea alba by maintaining a precise incision location. The posterior sheath is exposed and incised to begin developing a retro-rectus dissection, with attention to avoid entering the hernia sac for optimal visualization.

The surgeon enters the peritoneal sac while working towards the peri-umbilical hernia, revealing omentum within the hernia sac. The large retromuscular dissection previously developed maintains clear visualization. The surgeon proceeds to dissect and reduce the multifocal hernia defect.

The surgeon carefully maintains the posterior sheath, hernia sac, and peritoneum during the dissection to facilitate visceral sac reconstruction. The dissection progresses six to seven centimeters below the inferior aspect of the hernia, followed by completing the retro muscular dissection on the contralateral side, removing all areolar tissue.

The surgeon carefully reflects neurovascular elements towards the anterior abdominal wall while staying close to the posterior sheath. The hernia and diastasis are visible as the superior dissection is extended by two to four centimeters. Anterior fascial closure is performed through plication of the midline both below and above the defect for five to seven centimeters, re-approximating the muscle bellies. The hernia defect is closed using a number one non-absorbable suture, with the superior and inferior plication bringing the defect together with minimal tension.

The surgeon completes the sequential tightening of sutures, successfully re-approximating the midline and reconstructing the linea alba. The retromuscular space is measured, and a macroporous polypropylene mesh is strategically prepared for placement. Although not shown, the posterior eye is closed using a two-low absorbable V-lock suture.

The surgeon measures and rolls out a large mesh, approximately twenty-three by twenty-six centimeters, ensuring it lays flat within the surgical field. Tissio is applied, and a retromuscular drain is placed to facilitate postoperative recovery.
